coredata.dc:title: Effects of carbon quantum dots (CQD) on the energy storage capacity of a novel synthesized short-chain dyad
coredata.dc:creator: Mitra, I.
Paul, S.
Bardhan, M.
Das, S.
Saha, M.
Saha, A.
Ganguly, T.
coredata.dc:description: The present paper deals with the photophysical and time resolved spectroscopic investigations performed on the pristine short chain dyad, (E)-4-(((9H-fluorene-2-yl)imino)methyl)-N,Ndimethylaniline (NNDMBF) and its nanocomposite forms with CQD or NCQD. The experimental results with dyad-CQD (or NCQD) demonstrate that more than 80% trans structure of the ground state dyad could be protected in the environment of CQD (or NCQD) even on photoexcitation. Formations of elongated trans- conformers show in favor of efficient energy-storage systems. The stability of extended conformation has been hinted to be due to combination of surface coverage and surface trap effects, the later process seems to be the dominating one.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nanocomposites
Time resolved spectroscopy
Trans-cis conversion
Short-chain dyad
Light-energy converter

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis absorption and steady state fluorescence spectral measurements
Fluorescence lifetime determinations by TCSPC techniques
Transient absorption spectra and decays from laser flash photolysis measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Trans-structure and Cis-structure, (b) HRTEM image of CQD (Scale bar-10 nm).

UV–vis absorption spectra of the dyad NNDMBF (conc. ∼ 10−5 M in ACN) in presence of CQD of conc. (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M at the ambient temperature. (inset) Fluorescence CT emission spectra (exc ∼ 370 nm) of dyad NNDMBF in presence of CQD of conc. in (1) 0, (2) 0.84 × 10−4 M, (3) 1.68 × 10−4 M, (4) 3.36 × 10−4 M at the ambient temperature.

Fluorescence decay of the dyad NNDMBF when combined with CQD (red) in ACN (ex = 375 nm, em = 480 nm), the fast (blue) decaying component represents impulse response (diode laser) (χ2 ∼ 1.156). The residual is also shown. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence excitation spectra of dyad-CQD system (monitoring wavelength at 465 nm). (Inset) Fluorescence excitation spectra of the pristine dyad (monitoring wavelength at 465 nm).

Transient absorption spectra of NNDMBF-CQD nanocomposite system in ACN at different delay times (shown in 1: 0.57 µs and 2:1.97 µs). (inset in left) Transient absorption spectra of the pristine dyad NNDMBF in ACN at different delay times (shown in 1: 0.58 µs and 2: 1.98 µs). (inset in right) Comparative analysis of the transient absorption decays of (1) pristine dyad NNDMBF (r2 ∼ 0.99), (2) the transient absorption decay of dyad NNDMBF in presence of CQD (r2 ∼ 0.99) and (3) the transient absorption decay of dyad NNDMBF in presence of NCQD (r2 ∼ 0.99) at 540 nm monitoring wavelength which corresponds to acceptor (Fluorene) anionic species.

Fluorescence lifetime data of the short chain dyad NNDMBF in absence (pristine) and presence of CQD and NCQD by using 375 nm excitation (laser diode) and at various emission wavelengths at the ambient temperature. Values in parentheses besides lifetimes correspond to fractional contributions of the particular species in the total steady state fluorescence emission intensity (Temperature: 296 K).
Data obtained from the transient absorption decays at the ambient temperature. kCR (charge recombination rates) values are shown in parentheses. τ1 and τ2 are ion-pair lifetimes (∼τip = 1/kCR).

head.ce:title: Effects of carbon quantum dots (CQD) on the energy storage capacity of a novel synthesized short-chain dyad

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jadavpur University, Kolkata 700032, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Central Scientific Services, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Jadavpur, Kolkata 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces, Department of Chemistry, Xiamen University, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Center, III/LB-B Bidhannahgar, Kolkata 700 098, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transient absorption decay of 1: pristine dyad, 2: dyad-CQD and 3: Dyad-NCQD.

The present paper deals with the photophysical and time resolved spectroscopic investigations performed on the pristine short chain dyad, (E)-4-(((9H-fluorene-2-yl)imino)methyl)-N,Ndimethylaniline (NNDMBF) and its nanocomposite forms with CQD or NCQD. The experimental results with dyad-CQD (or NCQD) demonstrate that more than 80% trans structure of the ground state dyad could be protected in the environment of CQD (or NCQD) even on photoexcitation. Formations of elongated trans- conformers show in favor of efficient energy-storage systems. The stability of extended conformation has been hinted to be due to combination of surface coverage and surface trap effects, the later process seems to be the dominating one.

ce:section.ce:para: In our earlier communication [1], where we reported our studies on the short-chain dyad (E)-4-(((9H-fluorene-2-yl)imino)methyl)-N,Ndimethylaniline (NNDMBF) (Fig. 1 ) in its pristine form and when combined with gold nanoparticles (spherical and star-shaped:two morphologies of gold), trans-type (elongated nature) conformation was found to be more stable than cis-form (folded type) in the ground state of the pristine dyad. On photoexcitation trans form is converted into cis conformation showing this pristine dyad is of photoswitchable in nature [2–7]. Though, in pristine form, due to excitation trans-form is directly converted into cis configurartion but when the dyad becomes nanocomposite systems being combined with gold nanoparticles, some ground state trans forms still retain their identity even in the excited state showing possibility of slowing down energy destructive charge recombination processes within the redox components of the dyad. Out of the two gold nanoparticles, the spherical gold nanosurface possesses larger surface coverage area for the dyad relative to the other star surface shaped nanoparticles. The experimental evidences demonstrate that due to lack of space for adsorption of the dyad on the star gold surface the photoconversion of the dyad is hindered to some extent from its ground trans-form to excited cis form. The retention of the trans form of the dyad in case of star gold nanosurface, even in the excited state, makes this nanocomposite dyad system better candidate for building up of artificial light energy converter [8–17]. It is known that in trans-configuration, the two redox components, donor NNDMB and the acceptor fluorene (F), being far apart from each other suffer less overlapping minimizing the energy recombination processes. This will result in increased longevity of the charge-separated species. Our present investigations would be to examine whether carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and ethylenediamine (EDA) functionalized CQD (NCQD) could further enhance the ability of formation or retentions of trans-isomers in the excited state of the dyad so that the dyad-CQD (or dyad-NCQD) systems could behave as better light energy conversion devices relative to dyad-gold nanocomposites. Special attention was given to use CQD in nanocomposite dyad systems as it possesses wide range of applications due to their low cost of preparation and favorable properties such as chemical inertness, biocompatibility, non-toxicity and solubility in aqueous medium. In the present article the results, obtained by using UV–vis absorption, steady state fluorescence and time resolved spectroscopic techniques, are described and have been analyzed to explore the suitability for constructions of artificial light energy conversion and energy storage systems using the present dyad-CQD as well as dyad-NCQD nanocomposites.

The method of synthesis and characterization of the short chain dyad (E)-4-(((9H-fluorene-2-yl)imino)methyl)-N,Ndimethylaniline (NNDMBF) are described elsewhere [1]. The solvents acetonitrile (ACN) (SRL), and cyclohexane (CH) (Sigma Aldrich) of spectroscopic grade were purified following the standard procedures and tested before use for the absence of any impurity emission in the concerned wavelength region. Water was deionized using a Millipore Milli-Q system. The solutions were prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount of dyad in ACN and CH.

UV–vis absorption and steady state fluorescence emission spectra of dilute solutions (10−4–10−6 M) of the dyad were recorded at the ambient temperature (296 K) using 1 cm path length rectangular quartz cells by means of an UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (JASCO UV–Vis absorption spectrometer, Model: V-630) and JASCO spectrofluorimeter Model: 8200) respectively.

Fluorescence lifetimes were determined by using a time correlated single-photon-counting (TCSPC) technique with the model FLUOROLOG TCSPC HORIBA JOBIN YVON using nanosecond laser of 375 nm (Horiba scientific, DD-375L) as excitation source profile. The decay kinetics are monitored at emission wavelengths of 480 nm and 550 nm. The quality of fit is assessed over the entire decay, including the rising edge, and tested with a plot of weighted residuals and other statistical parameters e.g., the reduced χ2 and the Durbin–Watson (DW) parameters.
Nanosecond laser flash photolysis (Applied Photophysics) containing Nd:YAG laser (Lab series, Model Lab 150, Spectra physics) was used to measure transient absorption decays.
All the solutions prepared for room temperature measurements were deoxygenated by purging with an argon gas stream for about 30 min.

CQDs are synthesized from citric acid by microwave-assisted method [18]. 1 g of citric acid is dissolved in 10 mL of water, and the solution is heated in a domestic microwave oven (Whirlpool) at 1200 W power for ∼7 min. During microwave heating, the solution color is changed from colorless to pale yellowish brown which indicates the formation of CQD. The obtained product is dispersed in Millipore water and then centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 1 h) to remove large size particles. Finally the supernatant was collected carefully and purified asmentioned in the literature and pure CQD obtained are used for experimental studies. The average diameter was observed to be ∼2–5 nm (Fig. 1b). For the synthesis of amine-functionalized CQD (NCQD), 500 μL of EDA is added into 10 mL of solution (1 g of citric acid dissolved in 10 mL of water) [17]. The remaining procedure for preparation of NCQD is similar to the preparation of CQD. The sizes of CQD and NCQD are determined by using High Resolution Transmission electron Microscopy (HRTEM), carried out on a FEI, Tecnai G2 F30, S-Twin transmission electron microscope operating at 300 KV.

It has been inferred from the theoretical predictions made in our earlier paper [1], the dyad NNDMBF though in its ground state possesses both cis- and trans-forms but relatively trans-form (elongated nature) appears to be more stable. This prediction reflects in the UV–vis spectra of the pristine dyad NNDMBF where a strong charge transfer (CT) band corresponding to the stable trans-isomer was observed at 375 nm and a very weak tail, which may be due to another conformation “cis” of the dyad, is extended down to 450–500 nm region (Fig. 2 ). However, to corroborate this presumption fluorescence lifetime measurements (vide infra) by TCSPC technique were made which has been discussed below.

Anticipating quantum dots may form more efficient artificial light energy conversion nanocomposite devices than gold nanoparticles [1] owing to much smaller size (∼2.5 nm), the present investigations were performed with CQDs as well as NCQDs.

1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on Bruker AVIII-500. Chemical shifts were reported in ppm. Coupling constants (J values) were reported in Hertz. 1H NMR chemical shifts were referenced to CDCl3.13C NMR chemical shifts were referenced to CDCl3.
We have got exclusively trans isomer in 1H NMR, where chemical shift is 8.4 ppm in ground state. But on photoexcitation trans form is converted into cis conformation, which we haven’t isolated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and steady state fluorescence spectral measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The dyad NNDMBF shows a broad CT absorption band in ACN medium at 375 nm region and a very weak tail extending down to 450–550 nm region. With gradual addition of CQDs (or NCQDs) though lowering of absorption intensity was observed in 375 nm band but unlike the situations observed in case of gold nanoparticles [1] no development of 450 nm band of cis-conformer was observed (Fig. 2) at the concentrations of CQDs (or NCQDs) used in the present investigations. It may be presumed that the band corresponding to cis-form is too weak to be detectable. On excitation of 375 nm band of the pristine dyad, a broad and weak band envelop, presumably should be CT fluorescence, appear within the domain of 400–600 nm, peaking at around 462 nm (inset of Fig. 2). The band begins to augment with gradual addition of CQD (or NCQD). This band enhances uniformly throughout the spectra showing a single species is primarily involved in the production of fluorescence band. Thus in presence of CQDs and NCQDs most of the ground state trans-species of the nanocomposite dyad appear to be retained (only a few converts to cis) in the excited state. So from steady state measurements it shows that dyad-CQDs or dyad-NCQDs nanocomposites seem to be better artificial light energy storage systems as this particular conformation (elongated where the donor and acceptor redox components reside far apart from each other) prevents significantly energy destructive charge recombination process. However, to get corroborative evidences fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed by using TCSPC techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence lifetime determinations by TCSPC techniques
ce:section.ce:para: In cases of both dyad-CQDs and dyad-NCQDs nanocomposites, whatever be the region (480 nm or 550 nm) of the fluorescence spectra could be chosen as monitoring wavelengths, ns species (trans-isomer) dominates significantly (more than 80%, as evidenced from the computed fractional contribution, f, Table 1) over the cis-species having of ps lifetimes. This observation corroborates our views made from the steady state fluorescence emission spectra that primarily a single species is responsible for the observed fluorescence band of the dyad in presence of carbon quantum dots. From fluorescence excitation spectra of the dyad-CQD (or NCQD) nanocomposite systems it appears that whatever be the monitoring wavelengths 462 nm or 550 nm (Fig. 4 ), the excitation spectra shows significantly large peak at around 375 nm. This observation seemingly indicates that the ground state trans-species is primarily responsible for the origin of the fluorescence of the corresponding system. Time resolved fluorescence spectra clearly demonstrate further that the ground state trans-structure still retains its identity in the excited state. As this single species is of trans type structure, the charge recombination rates of the nanocomposites should be slowed down relative to the pristine dyad enhancing the ion-pair lifetimes (τip) of the former ones. This is exactly what was observed from the measurements of transient absorption spectra by laser flash photolysis techniques.
The results obtained from the transient absorption measurements are described in the following Section 3.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transient absorption spectra and decays from laser flash photolysis measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The transient absorption spectral measurements of the dyad-CQD (and NCQD) were made by using the third harmonic of Nd:YAG laser as excitation source (355 nm) at the different delay times of the excitation and analyzing or probing pulses. It is to be pointed out that the peaking of the transient absorption spectra observed at around 540 nm region could be assigned to the fluorene anion of the contact ion-pair, as confirmed earlier by Ganguly and Durocher [19]. The direct evidence of occurrence of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process is provided from the observations of transient donor cationic and acceptor anionic species. The transient spectra peaking at around 400 nm of the dyad NNDMBF in ACN correspond to donor cationic species (NNDMB+) (Fig. 5 and left inset of the same figure). This was confirmed from the production of the oxidized species of NNDMB (NNDMB+) artificially by constant current charger (model DB 300 DB Electronics, India). The excited singlet CT state of the dyad, 1(Dδ+-sp-Aδ−)* , initially formed by laser photoexcitation of the ground CT, will suffer nonradiative relaxation to form full electron transfer (ET) species, 1(D+-sp-A−)* in the excited singlet state.

This electron transfer (ET) possibility could be tested by using Rehm-Weller realtion [19,20]:
(1)
Δ
G
0
=
E
1 / 2
O X
D /
D
+
-
E
1 / 2
R E D
A
-
/ A
-
E
∗
where the symbols have their usual meanings. From the electrochemical measurements [4] the half-wave oxidation and reduction potential parameters were determined (EOX 1/2 (D/D+) ∼ +1.85 V, ERED 1/2 (A−/A) ∼ −1.02 V) and E* is chosen at 3.35 eV (370 nm). The driving energy, ΔG0 value was computed to be −0.48 eV. This shows there is a possibility from thermodynamic point of view the occurrence of electron transfer reactions within the dyad in the excited singlet state. Now, since the back ET for formation of the ground state is also largely possible from charge recombination mechanism as evidenced from negative value (−2.87 eV) of ΔGb(G), estimated from the relation,
Δ
G
b
G
= -
E
1 / 2
O X

D
+
+
E
1 / 2
R E D
A
-

" G "
f o r
g r o u n d
s t a t e
it appears that the formation of ground state dyad through charge recombination would be largely facilitated in case of pristine dyads (which have only cis structure in the excited state). The situation would be less favored in case of trans-structure of the excited dyad (due to elongated form, the redox partners will be far apart). Thus it seemingly indicates that the yield of charge separated states would be lower in case of pristine dyad and due to stability the charge separated yield will become larger in case of the nanocomposite dyads where the dyad combines with CQDs (or NCQDs). The findings of enhancement of fluorescence lifetimes of the dyad when it is bound to the CQDs corroborates the presumption. Thus the excited singlet ET state of the nanocomposite dyad, 1(D+-sp-A−)*, having trans-type conformation, populates the excited triplet state, 3(D+-sp-A−)* of charge separated species through nonradiative depletion processes via intersystem crossing (ISC). Fig. 5 reproduces the transient absorption spectra of the nanocomposite dyad systems (dyad/CQD) along with in the inset the transient spectra of the pristine dyad at the different delays of the exciting and probe pulses. The decrements in intensity of both the transients (donor cationic at 420 nm and acceptor anionic species at around 540 nm) species with increase of delay times (Fig. 5) demonstrate in favor of occurrence of charge or ion-pair recombination process. In Fig. 5 the transient absorption decays at the monitoring wavelength of 540 nm, where the acceptor anionic species F- absorbs, of the pristine dyad along with the dyad-CQD and dyad-NCQD nanocomposites have been shown, in the right inset of Fig. 5, for comparison. From the decays the ion-pair lifetimes (τip) along with the corresponding charge recombination rates (kCR) (approximately kCR ∼ 1/τip) were computed and shown in Table 2 . From Table 2 it is apparent that in case of the pristine dyad, the transient decay shows single exponential nature. On the other hand in the cases of the nanocomposites where the dyad adsorbs the nanosurfaces of CQD or NCQD, the decay becomes biexponential. From the computed fractional contributions, f, the preponderance of a particular conformer of the nanocomposite species, having 10 times larger ion-pair lifetimes (∼2μs, Table 2)in comparison to the pristine dyad (∼0.2 μs) is observed. From all the experimental evidences including fluorescence lifetimes indicate that trans-structure of the dyad in nanocomposites predominate in the excited state. This conformation is responsible for the formation of the longer-lived ion-pair than the pristine dyad which on photoexcitation turns from ground state trans isomer to folded configuration (cis-form) in the excited state. Larger energy wasting charge recombination rates with the concomitant reduction of the survival duration of the charge-separated species or ion-pair was observed in case of folded conformer. Table 2 shows the ion-pair lifetimes (τip) along with the corresponding charge recombination rates (kCR). From the observed f values it could be presumed that around 20% dyad remain unadsorbed in the excited state showing cis (folded) nature configuration whereas 80% of them being adsorbed on the surface of CQD (or NCQD) favor trans-form configuration. As in transient decay measurements excitation was made with the third harmonic of Nd: YAG laser (355 nm), where CQD at the concentrations used does not show any significant absorbance, no observable decay of CQDs alone was made.

If the results obtained from the dyad–gold nanocomposites are compared with dyad-CQD or dyad-NCQD, it is clearly observed that in the former case (using both spherical and star-shaped gold nanoparticles) maximum 60% of the ground state trans conformers could retain in the excited state [1]. From the present steady state and time resolved spectroscopic measurements it has been demonstrated that more than 80% (as evidenced from fluorescence lifetime determination) trans structure of the ground state dyad could be protected in the environment of CQD (or NCQD) even on photoexcitation. As trans- conformers facilitate the formation of longer-lived charge-separated species being, in general, of extended nature than the folded cis-form, the dyad-CQD and dyad-NCQD nanocomposites appear to be better light energy converter or energy-storage systems than the combination of the dyad-gold. One plausible cause for the retention of trans conformer even on photoexcitation in cases of nanocomposites (pristine dyad behaves differently) may be due to surface coverage [21,22] or surface trap [23] effect. In favor of proposition of conformational change due to surface coverage effect, the change in conformation (close packing) was found for polymeric chain systems [22] with the increase of the degree of the surface coverage area of the polymer on the nanoparticles. If we compare the particles dimensionality for the three nanocomposite systems e.g., dyad/spherical Au (Au ∼ 25 nm) [1], dyad/Au popcorn (Au popcorn ∼ 45 nm) [1] with dyad/CQD (CQD ∼ 2–5 nm), the largest amount (>80%) of the trans formation was observed due to photoexcitation when the dyad adsorbs the nanosurface of CQD (or NCQD) (Table 1).

It may be inferred that in the small size of a CQD (typically 2–5 nm) [24] a large fraction of its constituent atoms being on the surface may form the electronic states. These states may act as traps for electrons or holes, or both, localizing them at the surface. These trap states may be responsible to hinder the photoconversion of trans- to cis-isomer of the dyad as surface traps influence charge dynamics on a subnanosecond timescale. It appears that out of the two mechanisms the surface trapping appears to play dominant role in the formation of extended (trans) conformers. The QD surface is a highly dynamic region, coordinated by chemical species and exposed to the surrounding solvents that can have significant effects on the properties of the quantum dots [25]. These effects may lead to formations of localized electronic states or highly reactive sites, which are prone to chemical and redox processes. In these conditions, the formation of surface traps may stabilize the extended conformation of the dyad. However, the exact mechanisms are still not clear and to be explored. But the present experimental findings hint that the stability of conformation (trans form in CQD or NCQD) may be due to combination of surface coverage [21,22] and primarily due to surface trap [23,25] effects. In the trans conformation, the positioning of the atoms may force them closer together, increasing the amount of steric strain in the molecule. Further investigations with other similar nanocomposite dyads should be needed for the unequivocal assertion of this supposition.

The surface functional groups on the CQDs are capable of regulating the electron transfer within the redox components of the adsorbed dyad to affect its fluorescence intensity and lifetime properties. However in the present investigations the concentrations of CQD and NCQD that are used have both negligible absorbance. At such diluation, in comparison to the pristine dyad the increase in fluorescence lifetimes of the adsorbed dyad is observed to be of similar magnitudes (Table1) in presence of both ‘NCQD and the other quantum dot CQD. This observation demonstrates that at such low diluation amine functionalized CQDs exhibit a comparable conformation with CQD and a comparable trap density. Nevertheless, further investigations on similar dyad-QD nanocomposites are underway to reveal the exact mechanisms of formations of elongated conformers.

On comparing the experimental results of the dyad–gold nanocomposites with dyad-CQD (or NCQD), it is clearly observed that in the former case (using especially pop-corn or star-shaped gold nanoparticles) maximum 60% of the ground state trans conformers could retain in the excited state whereas more than 80% (as evidenced from fluorescence lifetime measurements) trans structure of the ground state dyad could be protected in the environment of CQD (or NCQD) even on photoexcitation. As trans- conformers facilitate the formation of longer-lived charge- separated species i.e., slower energy destructive charge recombination rates, the dyad-CQD (or NCQD) nanocomposites appear to be relatively better light energy converter or energy-storage systems. The hindrance of trans-cis conversion or stability of trans-structured species of nanocomposite dyad on photoexcitation appears to be due to joint effort of surface coverage and surface trap effects, the later one seemingly to be dominant. However, the exact mechanisms are yet to be explored. Possibility of increase of steric strain within the trans-structured dyad has been hinted for its stable conformation.
The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Tapan Ganguly gratefully acknowledges the financial support provided by the University Grants Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India for awarding Emeritus fellowship and research grant (F.6-6/2014-15/EMERITUS-2014-15-GEN-3976). MB expresses her gratitude to the DST-SERB project (YSS/2015/000589), DST, Govt of India. We like to thank Dr Madhurima Chakraborty, Vidyasagar College, Kolkata for fruitful discussion during preparation of the manuscript.
